content:
Inside your body, tiny cells work like busy factories. They need instructions to know what to do, just like you need a recipe to bake cookies. These instructions come from something called DNA, which is like a giant cookbook inside each cell. But DNA can’t leave the kitchen, so it makes a special helper called mRNA. Think of mRNA as a little messenger that copies one recipe from the cookbook and carries it out into the cell.

This messenger tells the cell how to make important things like proteins, which help your body grow and stay strong. The mRNA’s message is written in a special code, kind of like secret letters that the cell can read. Once the cell gets the message, it follows the instructions to build exactly what’s needed.

So, mRNA is like a helpful note that carries important instructions from the DNA cookbook to the cell’s workers, making sure everything runs smoothly!
